label: Linear relationship
claim: A linear relationship can be represented by a linear equation.
evidence: linear relationship

label: Linear equation
claim: A linear equation can be written to represent a situation.
evidence: write equations from real situations

label: Equation components
claim: The equation of a line in an experiment has two numbers and two variables.
evidence: The equation of the line in the experiment has two numbers and two variables.

label: Slope meaning
claim: The slope of the line in this situation has a specific meaning.
evidence: What does the slope mean in this situation?

label: Vertical intercept meaning
claim: The vertical intercept of the line has a specific meaning in this situation.
evidence: What does vertical intercept mean in this situation?

label: Linear Relationship
claim: A constant rate of change means there is a linear relationship between two quantities.
evidence: This constant rate of change means there is a linear relationship between the number of marbles and the height of the water.

label: Slope as Rate of Change
claim: The number 3 represents the rate of change, or slope of the graph.
evidence: The 3 represents the rate of change, or slope of the graph

label: Vertical Intercept
claim: The 50 represents the initial amount, or vertical intercept of the graph.
evidence: the 50 represents the initial amount, or vertical intercept of the graph

label: Calculating Slope from Points
claim: The slope of a line is the quotient of the length of the vertical side of the slope triangle and the length of the horizontal side of the slope triangle.
evidence: The slope of this line is the quotient of the length of the vertical side of the slope triangle and the length of the horizontal side of the slope triangle.

label: Equation Form for Linear Relationships
claim: Any linear relationships can be expressed in the form using just the rate of change, and the initial amount.
evidence: Any linear relationships can be expressed in the form using just the rate of change, and the initial amount

label: Equation as a balanced hanger
claim: An equation represents a balanced hanger where expressions on either side have equal value.
evidence: We can think of a balanced hanger as a metaphor for an equation. An equation says that the expressions on either side have equal value, just like a balanced hanger has equal weights on either side.

label: Maintaining balance in equations
claim: Adding or subtracting the same amount from both sides of an equation keeps the sides equal.
evidence: adding or subtracting the same amount from both sides of an equation keeps the sides equal to each other.

label: Solving linear equations
claim: Using systematic moves on both sides of an equation can find the value of a variable, such as x.
evidence: Using these moves in systematic ways, we can find that x=-1 is a solution to this equation.

label: System of linear equations
claim: A system of equations is a set of two or more equations where the letters represent the same values.
evidence: A system of equations is a set of 2 (or more) equations where the letters represent the same values.

label: Linear Equation for Distance
claim: The distance traveled by an object moving at a constant speed can be represented by an equation of the form d = rt, where d is distance, r is rate (speed), and t is time.
evidence: The distance in miles it has traveled from the rest area after t hours is d=75t.

label: Solving for Intersection Point
claim: To find a time when two objects are at the same distance from a reference point, you set their distance equations equal to each other and solve for the time variable.
evidence: Since we are asking when the d values for each car will be the same, we are asking for what t value, if any, makes 75t=14−65t true.

label: Example of Solving Linear Equation
claim: Solving the equation 75t = 14 - 65t yields a solution of t = 0.1 hours, meaning both cars are 7.5 miles away at that time.
evidence: Solving this equation for t, we find that t=0.1 is a solution and at that time the cars are 7.5 miles away since 75t=75⋅0.1=7.5.

label: Meeting Point Problem Setup
claim: When two objects are moving toward each other or in the same direction, finding when they meet involves setting their distance equations equal to each other (when they have traveled the same total distance).
evidence: To find when Lin and Diego meet, that is, when they have traveled the same total distance, we can set the two equations equal to one another: 18t=12(t+14).

label: Solving Meeting Time Example
claim: Solving the meeting equation 18t = 12(t+14) results in t = 12, meaning they meet after Diego rides for one half hour and Lin rides for three quarters of an hour.
evidence: Solving this equation for t, 18t=12t+3 6t=3 t=12 They meet after Diego rides for one half hour and Lin rides for three quarters of an hour.

label: Balanced Hanger Concept
claim: If we have equal weights on the ends of a hanger, then the hanger will be in balance.
evidence: If we have equal weights on the ends of a hanger, then the hanger will be in balance.

label: Unbalanced Hanger Concept
claim: If there is more weight on one side than the other, the hanger will tilt to the heavier side.
evidence: If there is more weight on one side than the other, the hanger will tilt to the heavier side.

label: Hanger as Equation Metaphor
claim: A balanced hanger can be thought of as a metaphor for an equation.
evidence: We can think of a balanced hanger as a metaphor for an equation.

label: Balancing Equation Concept
claim: Finding another pair of values that makes the hanger balance is a way to find a solution.
evidence: Can you find another pair of values that makes the hanger balance?

label: Equation definition
claim: An equation says that the expressions on each side have equal value.
evidence: An equation says that the expressions on each side have equal value

label: Balance analogy
claim: An equation is like a balanced hanger where the weights on each side are equal.
evidence: just like a balanced hanger has equal weights on each side

label: Maintaining equality by addition/subtraction
claim: Adding or subtracting the same amount from each side of an equation maintains the equality.
evidence: adding or subtracting the same amount from each side of an equation maintains the equality

label: Manipulating equations
claim: We can perform adding or subtracting the same amount from each side of an equation.
evidence: We can do these moves with equations as well: adding or subtracting the same amount from each side of an equation maintains the equality
